items.reality: Local development with structured logs at DEBUG is faster than print + rerun, because you can grep and filter. Use the same logger in dev and prod; only the level changes.

items.myth: `logging.info` is fast; you can log anywhere.
items.reality: Each log call has overhead — message formatting, filter chains, handler I/O. On hot paths (per token, per chunk), DEBUG logs need to be guarded or kept off in production.

items.myth: Plain-text logs are fine; JSON is overkill.
items.reality: On one machine, fine. The moment you have multiple processes / pods / containers, JSON + a log store is the difference between 5-minute and 5-hour investigations.
items.example: Datadog/Splunk/Loki all parse JSON natively; plain text degenerates into regex-mining.

items.reality: More *unstructured* logs = less visibility. The signal-to-noise ratio matters more than the absolute volume. Aim for: every state transition INFO, every recoverable surprise WARNING, every failure ERROR — and DEBUG for everything else only when needed.
items.example: A team logging 1M lines/day with no levels finds *nothing*. A team logging 50K with correct levels finds the root cause in 2 minutes.

items.reality: It only attaches a traceback when called *inside* an `except` block (it reads `sys.exc_info()`). Outside `except`, pass `exc_info=True` explicitly with the captured exception.
